food_subgroup: Crustaceans — whole lobster, grilled or steamed

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lobster food product class; no specific entry for a two-piece combo preparation. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers lobster as a commodity. No specific combo-preparation code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 identifies lobster as a substance. No specific prepared-combo code exists; closest parent used.

serving.description: 2 whole lobsters (500–600 g each), approximately 1000–1200 g combined raw weight; edible yield approximately 500–600 g cooked meat and shell
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is two whole lobsters each weighing 500–600 g, typically shared between two to four diners as part of a seafood meal.

nutrient_source.title: Lobster, cooked, moist heat (NDB No. 15147)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central — SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked lobster (moist heat) used as the primary nutrient basis per 100 g edible portion. The restaurant preparation (grilled/BBQ with garlic-butter sauce) may add marginal fat and sodium; values are adjusted upward for sodium (+30 mg/100 g) and fat (+1 g/100 g) to reflect typical restaurant seasoning. Core macros remain closely aligned with the USDA cooked-lobster reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for whole cooked lobster. Lobster is a high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate food (0.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g). By analogy with other high-protein, negligible-carbohydrate seafoods (e.g., shrimp, fish fillet) documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 and Brand-Miller et al. peer-reviewed GI tables, a GI of approximately 5 is assigned. Glycemic load per 550 g serving = (5 × 2.75 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.1, effectively negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from restaurant seasoning and sauces
hypertension.guidance: Each 100 g serving of restaurant-prepared lobster provides approximately 450 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sauce preparation to keep total sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lobster contains less than 1 g of carbohydrate per 100 g and has a very low glycemic index (~5), making it a suitable high-protein, low-glycemic option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided accompanying sauces are low in added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol and sodium from preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lobster contains approximately 145 mg cholesterol per 100 g and is relatively low in saturated fat (0.5 g/100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and choose steamed over butter-heavy preparations to limit saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Combo Lobster 2 Pieces (龙虾套餐 2只) is a Singaporean Chinese seafood dish consisting of two whole live lobsters, each weighing 500–600 g, prepared by grilling, steaming, or BBQ-style cooking with garlic, butter, soy sauce, and spring onion. The dish is a signature offering at Singaporean seafood restaurants such as The Seafood Market (by House of White Bee Hoon). The flesh is firm, sweet, and succulent with a mild oceanic flavour, and the shell turns bright red upon cooking. Nutritionally, lobster is an excellent source of lean protein (~20.5 g/100 g), providing all essential amino acids, and is rich in selenium (59 mcg/100 g), zinc (3.4 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (1.4 mcg/100 g), and phosphorus (280 mg/100 g). Fat content is low (~2.5 g/100 g) with minimal saturated fat. Carbohydrate content is negligible (<1 g/100 g), giving the dish a very low glycemic index (~5) and making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic-friendly meal plans. The primary clinical concern is sodium, which can reach 450 mg/100 g in restaurant preparations due to sauces and seasoning. Cholesterol content (~145 mg/100 g) is moderate and should be considered by individuals with cardiovascular disease. The dish is typically shared between two to four diners and is priced at a premium (~SGD 68 for two lobsters) reflecting the high cost of live seafood in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a whole lobster?
Can I eat lobster with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked lobster (moist heat), which is a well-validated reference for lobster flesh composition. Adjustments for restaurant seasoning (sodium, fat) are estimated. No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry exists for this exact restaurant combo preparation. GI is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed tables for high-protein, negligible-carbohydrate seafoods. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Serving size set to 550 g as the midpoint of the stated 500–600 g per lobster range for a single lobster (per-100g values apply to edible cooked flesh). The combo is two lobsters; the per_100g values represent the edible cooked portion. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy lobster entry. 3. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy data for cooked lobster; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. Sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; lobster flesh contains no free sugars. 5. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; lobster is an animal product with no dietary fibre. 6. Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked lobster. 7. Glycemic index set to 5 (analogous estimate for high-protein, negligible-carbohydrate seafood); gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 8. Halal status is marked false as lobster is not universally accepted as halal in all Islamic jurisprudence schools; consumers should verify with the restaurant. 9. The cuisine_tag 'Crab' in the input appears to be a platform tagging artefact; this record is for lobster, not crab. 10. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all sourced from USDA SR Legacy NDB 15147 cooked lobster.

Added GI analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008. Documented all zero values and -1 sentinel values in notes.

Verified all match_type values are within allowed set. Verified zero-macro justification phrases present in notes. Confirmed vitamin_d_mcg = -1 documented in notes.
